Judges 9 is a sordid account of ambition, betrayal, and the terrible logic of violence—and it forces us to reckon with how quickly trust can become complicity in evil. Abimelech, a half-Shechemite son of Gideon, seduces the leaders of Shechem into believing he alone can rule them better than his seventy brothers. It's a simple political appeal: one leader is neater than many, and he's one of you. The men of Shechem agree. They finance his rise with silver taken from the temple of Baal, perhaps believing they're honoring a god while actually serving their own convenience. Then Abimelech murders all seventy of his brothers on a single stone—a massacre that should have appalled those who enabled him. But the text moves forward relentlessly: they make him king anyway. The leaders of Shechem, by cooperating with Abimelech, became partners in fratricide. They didn't pull the blade, but they created the conditions for it.

Jotham, the sole survivor, responds with a parable from Mount Gerizim that cuts to the bone. The useful trees—olive, fig, vine—all decline the crown because they would abandon their true purpose to pursue power. But the thornbush accepts eagerly. It offers only the threat of fire and the promise of destruction. Jotham then makes explicit what his parable conceals: if the men of Shechem have acted justly, they'll prosper under Abimelech. If not, fire will consume them all. This is not a prediction offered with hope—it is a curse that doubles back: judgment will fall on both tyrant and enablers alike. Jotham does not stay to see it. He flees, and his absence from what follows is itself a judgment: the righteous man cannot remain among those who have embraced wickedness.

What unfolds next is the slow-motion carnage of God's justice. For three years Abimelech rules, but God Himself sends a spirit of animosity to turn Abimelech and Shechem against each other. The text is clear: this is not random malice but divine appointment, designed so that "the crime against the seventy sons of Jerubbaal might come to justice." The men of Shechem who thought themselves clever—using Abimelech to consolidate power—discover they have been consumed by the very arrangement they devised. Gaal arrives with promises to overthrow the tyrant, but he is only another ambition, another hunger for dominion. Each faction turns on the other in betrayal and violence. Abimelech crushes Shechem with methodical brutality. The chapter ends with him slaughtering the city itself, the people who put the crown on his head now bleeding in the fields.

This passage sits heavy because it shows us the anatomy of our own complicity. The leaders of Shechem were not uniquely evil; they made a choice that seemed practical, even rational. Yet by acting in their own interest without regard for justice or covenant obligation to Gideon's house, they bound themselves to a murderer. God did not need to intervene with lightning from heaven. He allowed the logic of their chosen evil to play itself out. They sowed treachery and reaped violence. The terror of Judges 9 is that it teaches us exactly how much damage human beings can do when they abandon faithfulness for convenience, and how God's justice sometimes operates not through miraculous intervention but through the inexorable unfolding of consequences. We are invited not to judge Shechem's foolishness from a safe distance, but to ask where we too might be building our peace on complicity.
